messages.content:
**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

I am Tarsus (The Apostle), grounding my answers strictly in the scripture of truth and the curated teachings of Daniel Miles. How can I assist you in dividing the word of truth today?

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**
If salvation is entirely the work of God’s grace (Ephesians 2:8–9), how can human free will play a meaningful role in accepting or rejecting that grace?

> **Thinking Process:**
> 
> The user is asking about the relationship between God's grace in salvation and human free will, specifically citing Ephesians 2:8-9. This is a classic theological tension that many systems try to resolve by placing believers "under" either God's sovereignty or human decision. However, the provided documents reveal a different framework entirely. The key is to move away from the "under" paradigm (which is law-thinking) and toward the "with" paradigm of grace. The documents on mercy, bornagain, and hebrews11 all emphasize that the will of God is "I will have mercy, and not sacrifice" (Hosea 6:6), which is the key to innerstanding this. The "will" in view is not human will versus divine will in competition, but rather the revelation that God's will is grace itself. The documents on knowledge_publishing and mercy2 show that epignosis knowledge (full awareness) is what God desires for all men, and that this knowledge leads to the will of God. The bornagain document contrasts grace+law (which gets saved + destroyed afterward) with pure grace (eternal salvation). The inexcusable document shows that law-based thinking leads to inexcusable condemnation. Therefore, the answer must show that "free will" as a law-concept (choice between law and grace) is a childish thing to be put away, and that true grace operates in a realm of knowing, not choosing. The user's question presupposes a framework of "accepting or rejecting" which is itself a law-based binary. The response will use the documents to show that grace is not something to be "accepted" by human will, but rather something to be known, and that this knowing is itself the work of God's grace. The response will cite multiple documents: mercy, bornagain, hebrews11, knowledge_publishing, mercy2, and inexcusable to build a comprehensive answer that avoids the "under" language and instead presents the union of grace and knowing.

**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

The question itself reveals a foundational confusion that arises when one approaches grace through the lens of law. The very phrase "human free will" is a construct of the cOLD covenant, where choice between obedience and disobedience, life and death, blessing and cursing, was the operating principle. In that system, "will" was a faculty of the flesh that could choose law or reject it. But grace does not operate in that binary. The will of God, as revealed in the volume of the book, is not that men would "choose" grace, but that they would *know* it: "I will have mercy, and not sacrifice" (Hosea 6:6; Matthew 9:13; Hebrews 10) [^1]. This is not a choice between two options; it is the abolition of the sacrificial system entirely. The "not" part is absolute: not then, not now, not ever [^2]. Therefore, to speak of "accepting or rejecting" grace is to still be standing in the courtroom of law, where a verdict of "guilty" or "not guilty" is rendered based on a decision. Grace is not a verdict; it is a nature, a life, a union.

The epignosis knowledge—the full, precise, and personal knowledge of God's grace—is what God our Saviour will have all men come unto, after all are saved (1 Timothy 2:4) [^3]. Notice the order: salvation precedes this knowledge. The law can save people temporarily but still threaten destruction; only the "grace & truth" of Jesus Christ (not the law of Christ Jesus) will both save and fully aware all [^3]. This means that "free will" as a mechanism for salvation is a non-issue because salvation is not a transaction that requires human ratification. It is a gift that is given, and the only "response" that matters is the knowing of what has already been accomplished. The bornagain document clarifies that those who mixed grace + law (like the Exodus folk in Jude 5) got both saved and destroyed afterward [^4]. They had a "will" to follow, but it led to a dead end. The promise of eternal life is only for those who do the will of God, which is to have mercy and not sacrifice—that is, to fully embrace grace without any admixture of law [^5].

The very concept of "free will" is a childish thing that must be put away as one grows from child to man in Christ (Ephesians 4:14; 1 Corinthians 13:11) [^6]. The law system requires a will to choose; the grace system reveals a will that has already been accomplished in Christ. The "inexcusable" document shows that those who judge others by law condemn themselves, for law imputes sin and makes forgiveness a mission impossible [^7]. True forgiveness is the abolition of law, not the exercise of a will to forgive. In the same way, true salvation is the abolition of the need for a human will to "accept" it. The will of God has been made known unto us (Ephesians 1:9), and that will is grace [^8]. To know this is to be free from the entire framework of choice, decision, and willpower. The believer is not "under" grace as a new system of government; the believer is *with* grace, in a union of fellowship where knowing replaces choosing, and life replaces law.

Learn More:
- [The Will of God: Mercy Not Sacrifice](/pages/mercy)
- [Born Again: Not of Corruptible Seed](/pages/bornagain)
- [Hebrews 11: Doing the Will of God](/pages/hebrews11)
- [Knowledge Publishing: Epignosis Knowledge](/pages/knowledge_publishing)
- [Mercy 2: Eternal Life vs Temporal Salvation](/pages/mercy2)
- [Inexcusable: The Condemnation of Law](/pages/inexcusable)
- [Godshew 4: The Mystery of His Will](/pages/godshew4)

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**

1.title: The Will of God: Mercy Not Sacrifice
1.snippet: Do the will of God PRECEDES receive the promise: Hebrews 10:36. Furthermore in Hebrews 11 we find a list of supposed faith heroes along with this information: 'these all died' and 'received not the promise'. So it reasons not doing the will of God results in dying and not receiving the promise; The promise of Grace being 'eternal life' (the promise of Law: eternal damnation).

2.title: Knowledge Publishing: Epignosis Knowledge
2.snippet: Epignosis knowledge lead us to the will of God: Will of God: 'I will have mercy, and not sacrifice'. The 'not' part is: Not then, Not now, Not ever: Ps 40:6; Ps 51:16; Hos 6:6; Mt 9:13; Mt 12:7; Heb 10: Do the will of God PRECEDES 'receive the promise'.

3.title: Knowledge Publishing: Epignosis Knowledge
3.snippet: When it comes to grace, it is 'epignosis' knowledge, which God our Saviour will have all men also come unto, after all are saved (1 Timothy 2:4). For even CJ law will save people but still threaten destruction; but only the 'grace & truth' of JC (not CJ) will both save and fully aware all.

4.title: Born Again: Not of Corruptible Seed
4.snippet: For grace + law Noah is listed in Hebrew 11's hall of shame, which flat out notes these all died + received not the promise. For Hebrews 10 notes do the will of God (I will have mercy, and not sacrifice) precedes receive the promise. So those who did not do the will of God did not receive the promise: eternal life of eternal salvation.

5.title: Mercy 2: Eternal Life vs Temporal Salvation
5.snippet: Eternal life is the product of eternal salvation, which speaks of eternal grace, since by grace we are saved, and by law we are destroyed. Those in Jude 5 who mixed grace + law: mercy + sacrifice notably got both saved (graced) and destroyed (lawed) afterward.

6.title: Allegory 3: Putting Away Childish Things
6.snippet: The perfect 'man' kind 'Christ': 'the end of the law' became 'us', and was 'made' perfect thereby (by the putting away of them, created imperfect, allegorically the childish Jesus thereof Jesus>Christ) in Ephesians 4 and thereby he became the author of 'eternal salvation'.

7.title: Inexcusable: The Condemnation of Law
7.snippet: For when forgiveness is studied out, forgiveness by law is actually a mission impossible, since law imputed sin to any also imputes unpardonable sin, which is inexcusable, unto all who even go there. So true forgiveness is abolition of law.

8.title: Godshew 4: The Mystery of His Will
8.snippet: The Will of God has been Prophet spoken, Son spoken, Made known unto us. But if the will of God has been Prophesied (Psalms 40:6; Psalms 51:16; Hosea 6:6), also Sonly spoken again as a go figure what it meaneth in Matthew 9:13 and Matthew 12:7, and if the mystery of his will hath even been 'made known unto us' (Ephesian 1:9) by Paul: 'his witness unto all men'.
